1: ben-šeḇaʿ šānîm Yôʾāš bᵉmalᵉkô (‘Joash was seven years old when he became king’) — the youngest accession age recorded in the Hebrew Bible. His forty-year reign (ʾarbaʿîm šānāh mālaḵ bîrûšālāim) is one of the longest in Judah’s history. The mother’s name, Ziḇyāh miBᵉʾēr-šāḇaʿ, signals southern Negev origin; the name Ziḇyāh means ‘gazelle.’
2: wayyaʿaś Yôʾāš hayyāšār bᵉʿênê YHWH kol-yᵉmê Yᵉhôyādāʿ hakkōhēn (‘Joash did what was right in the sight of the LORD all the days of Jehoiada the priest’) — the qualification kol-yᵉmê Yᵉhôyādāʿ is the structural key to the chapter’s entire narrative arc: faithfulness conditioned on priestly mentorship, which fails when that mentorship ends (v17-18). The Chronicler flags Joash’s dependence from the outset, making the later apostasy narratively inevitable once the mentor dies.
3: wayyiśśāʾ-lô Yᵉhôyādāʿ nāšîm šetayim (‘Jehoiada chose two wives for him’) — the priest overseeing the king’s marriage arrangements reflects Jehoiada’s extraordinary guardianship role. Two wives (not the polygamous excess associated with Solomon’s downfall) is recorded without condemnation; the Chronicler notes sons and daughters as a sign of dynastic blessing. The verse functions as a quiet positive marker before the temple repair narrative.
4: wayhî ʾaḥᵃrê kēn hāyāh ʿim-lēḇ Yôʾāš lᵉḥaddēš ʾet-bêt YHWH (‘Joash decided to repair the LORD’s house’) — ḥādaš (H2318, Piel): to renew, restore, repair; the same verb for covenant renewal. hāyāh ʿim-lēḇ (‘it was in his heart’) — the idiom of purposed intention; Joash’s initiative is genuinely his own (not prompted here by Jehoiada). The temple repair is the first major narrative sequence.
5: liqqᵉṭ-kesep mikkol-Yiśrāʾēl lᵉḥazzēq ʾet-bêt-ʾĕlōhêḵem mišānāh bᵉšānāh (‘collect money from all Israel year by year to repair the house of your God’) — the annual collection reflects the Mosaic sanctuary tax (Exodus 30:12-16). wᵉʾattem tᵉmahᵃrû laddāḇār wᵉlōʾ mîharrû halᵉwiyyim (‘Do this promptly. But the Levites did not act promptly’) — māhar (H4116): to hurry, hasten; the same verb used positively and negatively in rapid sequence, highlighting the gap between royal command and Levitical execution.
6: maśśaʾt Mōšeh ʾeḇed-YHWH wᵉqahal Yiśrāʾēl (‘the collection ordered by Moses the servant of God and the assembly of Israel’) — the dual authority cited for the tax: Mosaic law and assembly ratification. miškan hāʿēḏût (‘the tent of testimony’) — the Exodus-era designation for the tabernacle; the Chronicler preserves the archaic name, connecting the contemporary collection to its Sinai origins. The king’s rebuke of Jehoiada is notable: even the chief priest is accountable to royal initiative in civil-religious matters.
7: kî ʿAtalyāhû hammiršaʿat bānêhā pāṣᵉṣû ʾet-bêt hāʾĕlōhîm (‘the sons of that wicked woman Athaliah had broken into the house of God’) — pāṣaṣ (H6555): to break open, break through; the rare verb for violent forced entry. hammiršaʿat (‘that wicked woman’) — the feminine form of rāšāʿ (wicked) used as an epithet; the Chronicler’s only direct personal pejorative for a named individual. The desecration by Athaliah’s sons — using dedicated things (qᵉdāšôt) for Baal — is the historical context for the urgent need for temple repair.
8: wayyōʾmēr hammelek wayyaʿᵃśû ʾarôn ʾeḥāḏ (‘at the king’s command they made a chest’) — ʾārôn (H727): chest, box; the same word for the ark of the covenant, but here a collection chest. The placement miḥûṣ lᵉšaʿar bêt-YHWH (‘outside the gate of the LORD’s house’) makes the collection maximally accessible to all who pass through. The public placement of the chest inverts the secrecy of the previous repair attempts.
9: wayyittᵉnû-qôl bîhûdāh ûḇîrûšālāim (‘they issued a proclamation throughout Judah and Jerusalem’) — nātan qôl (gave/raised a voice): a technical idiom for official public announcement. The proclamation references maśśaʾt Mōšeh ʾeḇed-hāʾĕlōhîm ʿal-Yiśrāʾēl bammidḇār (‘the contribution Moses the servant of God required of Israel in the wilderness’) — locating the current tax in the Sinai narrative gives it maximum covenantal legitimacy.
10: wayyiśmaḥû kol-haśśārîm wᵉḵol-hāʿām wayyāḇîʾû wayyašlîḵû lāʾārôn (‘all the princes and all the people rejoiced and brought their contributions, casting them into the chest’) — šālak (H7993, Hiphil): to throw, cast; ʿad-lᵉkallôt (‘until it was full’) — the enthusiastic response fills the chest repeatedly. The willing giving in contrast to the Levites’ dilatoriness (v5) demonstrates that popular enthusiasm exceeded institutional initiative.
11: bᵉʿēt yāḇîʾû ʾet-hāʾārôn ʾel-pᵉquddāt hammelek bᵉyad halᵉwiyyim (‘whenever the Levites brought the chest to the king’s officers’) — the oversight mechanism: royal secretary (sōpēr hammelek) and high priest’s officer jointly empty the chest and return it, creating a dual audit. wayyaʿᵃśû-ḵen yôm bᵉyôm (‘they did this day after day’) — repeated daily collection and oversight; the administrative regularity is an earnest of good governance.
12: wayyittᵉnûhû hammelek wîhôyādāʿ ʾel-ʿōśê mᵉlāʾḵat ʿăḇōdat bêt-YHWH (‘the king and Jehoiada gave the money to those who supervised the work on the LORD’s house’) — the project management is at the royal-priestly level, with skilled labor hired: ḥāṣᵉḇê ʾāḇānîm ûḇōnîm (stone-cutters and builders), ḥārāšê barzel ûnᵉḥōšet (ironworkers and bronzesmiths) — the same craftsmen categories as the original temple construction (cf. 2 Chronicles 2:7, 14).
13: wayᵉqîmû ʾet-habbayit ʾĕlōhîm ʿal-mattᵉkōnᵉtô (‘they restored the house of God to its proper condition’) — mattᵉkōnet (H8506): established condition, proper state; the temple is returned to its foundational standard. wayyᵉḥazzᵉqûhû (‘and reinforced it’) — ḥāzaq (H2388, Piel): to strengthen, reinforce; the Piel intensive suggesting thoroughgoing structural renewal, not minimal patching.
14: kᵉlê šērēt wᵉhaʿᵃlôt kappôt ûkᵉlê zāhāḇ wāḵesef (‘vessels for service and offerings, spoons and vessels of gold and silver’) — the liturgical equipment restored includes both functional vessels and precious decorative objects. The ongoing offering: wayyaʿᵃlû ʿōlôt bᵉḇêt-YHWH tāmîd kol-yᵉmê Yᵉhôyādāʿ (‘they offered burnt offerings in the LORD’s house continually all the days of Jehoiada’) — the tāmîd (perpetual offering) resumes as the sign of restored worship. The phrase kol-yᵉmê Yᵉhôyādāʿ again marks the temporal qualifier that v2 established as the hinge of Joash’s faithfulness.
15: wayyizqan Yᵉhôyādāʿ wayyiśbaʿ yāmîm wayyāmot ben-mʾāh ûšᵉlōšîm šānāh bᵉmôtô (‘Jehoiada grew old and full of years, and he died at the age of one hundred and thirty’) — śāḇaʿ yāmîm (‘full of years/days’) is the honorific formula used for Abraham (Genesis 25:8) and Job (Job 42:17). Jehoiada at 130 exceeds Moses (120, Deuteronomy 34:7) and Joshua (110, Joshua 24:29) — the only non-patriarchal figure given a longer lifespan in the OT. The longevity implicitly commends his faithfulness: long life as covenant blessing.
16: wayyiqbᵉrûhû bᵉʿîr Dāwîd ʿim-hamᵉlāḵîm (‘they buried him in the city of David among the kings’) — Jehoiada is buried with the Davidic kings, an honor reserved for no other priest in the Hebrew Bible. The reason: kî-ʿāśāh ṭôḇāh bᵉYiśrāʾēl ûḇiʾĕlōhîm ûḇᵉḇêtô (‘because he had served Israel well — both toward God and toward his house’). The tripartite summary of Jehoiada’s service — to Israel, to God, and to the temple — is the Chronicler’s fullest commendation of any non-royal figure.
17: wᵉʾaḥᵃrê môt Yᵉhôyādāʿ bāʾû śārê Yᵉhûdāh wayyišdᵃḥᵃwû lammelek (‘after Jehoiada’s death, the officials of Judah came and bowed before the king’) — the political shift is immediate: new advisors replace the priest. wayyikḇᵃdûhû (‘and honored him’) — kāḇad (H3513, Piel): to honor, make heavy/important; the officials flatter the king into receptivity for their idolatrous proposals. The structural parallel to Rehoboam’s rejection of older counsel (2 Chr 10) is deliberate: young kings who defer to a new advisory class abandon the wisdom of their fathers.
18: wayyaʿaḇᵉdû ʾet-hāʾăšērîm wᵉʾet-hāʿᵃṣabbîm (‘served the Asherah poles and idols’) — ʾăšērāh (H842): the sacred tree or pole associated with the Canaanite goddess; ʿᵃṣabbîm (H6091): images, idols. wayhî qeṣep ʿal-Yᵉhûdāh ûḇirûšālāim bᵉʾašmātām zōʾt (‘wrath came upon Judah and Jerusalem because of this guilt’) — qeṣep (H7110): wrath, indignation; ʾašmāh (H819): guilt, guilt-offering; the guilt of idolatry is the theological cause of the subsequent military defeat (vv23-24).
19: wayyišlaḥ bāhem nᵉḇiʾîm lᵉhāšîḇām ʾel-YHWH (‘yet he sent prophets among them to bring them back to the LORD’) — the pattern of prophetic warning before judgment is a structural constant in Chronicles: YHWH’s judgment is never without prior appeal. wayyāʿîdû bām wᵉlōʾ ʾāzānû (‘they testified against them, but they would not listen’) — ʿûd (H5749, Hiphil): to bear witness, testify against; ʾāzan (H238, Hiphil): to give ear, listen. The sequence: prophetic sending → warning → refusal establishes legal grounds for judgment.
20: wᵉrûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm lāḇᵉšāh ʾet-Zᵉḵaryāhû (‘the Spirit of God clothed Zechariah’) — lāḇaš (H3847): to clothe, put on; the Spirit-as-vestment idiom (cf. Judges 6:34 of Gideon; 1 Chronicles 12:18 of Amasai). The Spirit ‘clothing’ a person expresses total possession and prophetic commission. Zechariah son of Jehoiada is mentioned only here; his message: ʿăzaḇtem ʾet-YHWH wayyaʿaḇōḇ ʾetḵem (‘you have forsaken the LORD and he has forsaken you’) — ʿāzaḇ (H5800) repeated in chiastic structure: your abandonment produces divine abandonment.
21: wayyiqqᵉšᵉrû ʿālāyw wayyirgemûhû ʾeḇen bᵉmiṣwat hammelek (‘they conspired against him and stoned him to death at the king’s command’) — qāšar (H7194, Hithpael): to bind together, conspire; rāgam ʾeḇen (‘to stone’; H7275 + H68): stoning as mob execution. The location — bᵉḥăṣar bêt-YHWH (in the court of the LORD’s house) — makes the murder a desecration of the very sanctuary Zechariah’s father had restored and whose court Joash had filled with temple worship.
22: wᵉlōʾ-zāḵar Yôʾāš hammelek hahesed ʾăšer-ʿāśāh Yᵉhôyādāʿ ʾāḇîw ʿimmô (‘Joash did not remember the kindness that Jehoiada his father had shown him’) — zāḵar ḥeseḏ (‘remember kindness’): the failure of ḥeseḏ-memory (loyal-love recall) is the moral pivot of the narrative. Joash forgets what Jehoiada did for him (hiding him, crowning him, advising him for forty years). Zechariah’s dying words: yidroš YHWH wᵉyirʾeh (‘may the LORD see and avenge/require’) — dāraš (H1875) in its judicial sense: to seek, hold accountable; the Chronicler’s ironic use of the same root used for positive ‘seeking God’ (cf. 2 Chronicles 22:9).
23: ûlitᵉqûpat haššānāh (‘at the turn of the year’) — tᵉqûpāh (H8622): circuit, turn; the year’s end or the spring campaign season when armies traditionally marched (cf. 2 Samuel 11:1). wayyaʿal ḥêl ʾĂrām (‘the Syrian army came against him’) — the military punishment fulfilling the theological cause established in v18 (guilt/wrath). The scope of destruction: wayyišlaḥû ʾet-kol-šᵉlālām ʾel-melek Dammeśeq (‘sending all their plunder to the king of Damascus’) — Judah’s wealth stripped and transferred north.
24: kî bᵉmiṣʿar ʾănāšîm bāʾ ḥêl ʾĂrām ûḇᵉrōḇ ʾănāšîm nātan YHWH bᵉyādām ḥayil lārōḇ (‘though the Syrian army came with only a small force, the LORD handed a very great army over to them’) — the contrast of small force defeating large army is the Chronicler’s characteristic reversal: human military calculus is inverted by divine determination. kî ʿāzᵉḇû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇôtêhem (‘because Judah had abandoned the LORD’) — again ʿāzaḇ as the causal key; YHWH himself executes judgment: ûḇᵉYôʾāš ʿāśû šᵉpāṭîm (‘they executed judgment on Joash’).
25: ûbᵉlektām mimmennû ʿāzᵉḇû ʾōtô bimᵉḥālûyîm rabbîm (‘when the Syrians departed they left him severely wounded’) — maḥalûy (H4245): sickness, wound; plural of intensity. ʿal-dᵉmê ben-Yᵉhôyādāʿ hakkōhēn (‘because of the blood of the son of Jehoiada the priest’) — the explicit causal nexus: Zechariah’s blood requires retribution (dām as legal claim); the servants are instruments of justice. wayyiqbᵉrûhû bᵉʿîr Dāwîd wᵉlōʾ qᵉḇārūhû bᵉqiḇrôt hamᵉlāḵîm (‘buried in the city of David, but not in the tombs of the kings’) — the same posthumous dishonor as Jehoram (2 Chronicles 21:20), closing an inclusio of royal disgrace.
26: Zāḇāḏ ben-Šimᵉʾat hāʿamōnît wᵉYôzāḇāḏ ben-Šimrît haMôʾāḇît — the Chronicler specifies both the assassins’ names and the foreign ethnic identity of their mothers (hāʿamōnît, Ammonite; haMôʾāḇît, Moabite). The parallel in 2 Kings 12:21-22 records slightly different names (Jozachar and Jehozabad) and does not note the mothers’ ethnicity. The Chronicler’s addition of foreign maternal lineage underscores the bitter irony: the king who abandoned YHWH’s covenant was assassinated by the sons of foreign women — outsiders to the covenant community enacting judgment on a covenant-breaker.
27: ûḇānāyw wᵉrōḇ hammaśśāʾ ʿālāyw wᵉyissôd bêt hāʾĕlōhîm hinnām kᵉtûḇîm ʿal-midrash sēper hamᵉlāḵîm (‘as for his sons, the many oracles about him, and the rebuilding of the house of God — these are recorded in the commentary on the book of the kings’) — midrāš (H4097): the term appears only here and 13:22 in the Hebrew Bible; it derives from dāraš (to seek, inquire) and refers to an interpretive or expository commentary on an existing document. This is not the later rabbinic genre of Midrash but an early form of explanatory historiographical writing about royal history. The Chronicler cites it as a source document, suggesting a tradition of interpretive writing alongside the royal annals.
